_meta.title: Approximation Algorithms — Vertex Cover, TSP, Set Cover

questions.question: An algorithm has an approximation ratio of α = 3 for a minimization problem. If the optimal solution costs 100, what is the worst case cost returned by the algorithm?
questions.explanation: For minimization: cost(ALG) ≤ α · cost(OPT). With α = 3 and OPT = 100, the algorithm guarantees cost ≤ 3 × 100 = 300.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a PTAS and an FPTAS?
options: PTAS is always faster than FPTAS
FPTAS running time is polynomial in both n and 1/ε; PTAS running time may depend exponentially on 1/ε
PTAS can only approximate maximization problems
There is no difference; they are synonyms
questions.explanation: An FPTAS runs in time polynomial in both the input size n and 1/ε. A PTAS is only required to be polynomial in n for each fixed ε, meaning its runtime may grow exponentially as ε shrinks.

questions.question: In the 2-approximate vertex cover algorithm, why does the maximal matching approach guarantee |cover| ≤ 2|OPT|?
options: Because each vertex can cover at most 2 edges
Because OPT must pick at least 1 endpoint per matching edge (so |OPT| ≥ |M|), and the algorithm picks both endpoints (|cover| = 2|M|)
Because maximal matching always equals maximum matching
questions.explanation: The proof: (1) Every edge in the maximal matching M must have at least one endpoint in OPT, and since matching edges are disjoint, |OPT| ≥ |M|. (2) The algorithm returns 2|M| vertices. So |cover| = 2|M| ≤ 2|OPT|.

questions.question: The greedy set cover algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of O(log n). What property of the greedy choice makes this bound tight — why can't it do better in the worst case?
options: Greedy always picks the smallest set first
Adversarial instances force each greedy step to cover only a small fraction of remaining elements, accumulating log(n) × OPT cost
Greedy algorithms inherently cannot be optimal
The universe elements are always uniformly distributed
questions.explanation: Adversarial constructions (based on harmonic series) create instances where each greedy step picks a set that barely beats alternatives, covering a diminishing fraction of remaining elements. The total cost accumulates to H(n) ≈ ln(n), making the log(n) bound tight.

questions.question: What does the approximation ratio of an algorithm measure?
options: How close the algorithm's running time is to the optimal
The worst-case ratio between the algorithm's solution value and the optimal solution value
The probability of finding the exact optimal solution
The number of iterations the algorithm performs
questions.explanation: The approximation ratio (or factor) is the worst-case ratio between the cost of the approximate solution and the cost of the optimal solution. A 2-approximation for vertex cover means the algorithm always finds a cover at most twice the size of the minimum cover.
